locked_facts: Conduction: heat transfer through a medium without bulk motion — Q/t = KA(T₁-T₂)/L
Convection: heat transfer by bulk movement of fluid — natural (density-driven) or forced (fan/pump)
Radiation: heat transfer through electromagnetic waves — requires no medium
Stefan-Boltzmann law: power radiated P = eσAT⁴ (σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴)
Wien's displacement law: λ_max T = 2898 μm·K
Newton's law of cooling: dT/dt = -k(T - T_surroundings) for small temperature differences
A perfect blackbody has emissivity e = 1 and absorbs all incident radiation
truth_statements: Conduction transfers heat through molecular collisions without any bulk movement of the material
Convection requires a fluid medium and involves actual movement of the heated material
Radiation can transfer heat through a vacuum — it is the only mode that does not require a material medium
A body at temperature T radiates energy proportional to T⁴ — doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold
Good absorbers of radiation are also good emitters — this is Kirchhoff's law of radiation

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three demonstrations side by side: (1) Metal rod heated at one end — temperature wave propagates along it (conduction), (2) Water heated from below — convection currents visible with dye tracers, (3) Heat felt from a fire across vacuum (no medium) — radiation. Only radiation needs no medium.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three panels: molecular vibrations propagating (conduction), fluid circulation loops (convection), electromagnetic waves crossing vacuum (radiation)
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students conflate the three modes — seeing them simultaneously with their distinct mechanisms clarifies when each applies

simulation_states.label: Conduction
simulation_states.description: Metal rod heated at one end. Colour gradient shows temperature propagating. Q/t = KA(T₁-T₂)/L. Metals conduct well (high K), wood poorly (low K).
simulation_states.emphasis: Heat flows through solid by molecular vibrations — no bulk motion

simulation_states.label: Convection
simulation_states.description: Water heated from below. Warm water rises (less dense), cool water sinks. Convection cells form. Dye tracers show circulation pattern. Natural vs forced convection.
simulation_states.emphasis: Hot fluid rises, cold fluid sinks — bulk movement transfers heat

simulation_states.label: Radiation
simulation_states.description: Hot body emits EM waves. P = eσAT⁴. Doubling T → 16× radiation. Works through vacuum. All bodies above 0 K radiate. Wien's law: hotter → shorter wavelength.
simulation_states.emphasis: No medium needed. Power ∝ T⁴ — extremely sensitive to temperature.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts temperature and material properties. Conduction: change K and L. Convection: adjust heating rate. Radiation: T⁴ graph. Newton's cooling law demo.
simulation_states.emphasis: Double the temperature — radiation increases 16×. Change material — conduction rate changes.

common_misconceptions.correction: Heat itself doesn't rise — hot FLUID rises because it becomes less dense. This is convection. Conduction transfers heat in any direction (along the temperature gradient). Radiation travels in all directions.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show conduction transferring heat downward through a rod, disproving 'heat always rises'

common_misconceptions.belief: Radiation requires a medium like air
common_misconceptions.correction: Radiation is the only heat transfer mode that works through a vacuum. The Sun heats the Earth through the vacuum of space entirely by radiation. No particles are needed — electromagnetic waves carry the energy.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show radiation crossing a vacuum gap between two bodies — heat transfers without any medium
